name: United States Antimony Corporation

profile.description: United States Antimony Corporation produces and sells antimony, zeolite, and precious metals in the United States and Canada. It operates the only two antimony smelters in North America and positions itself as the first fully integrated antimony producer outside China. Products include antimony trioxide (flame retardants), antimony metal (batteries, ordnance), and antimony trisulfide (ammunition primers). Also produces zeolite for agricultural and industrial applications. Founded in 1968, headquartered in Dallas, Texas.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): UAMY has undergone a dramatic transformation from an obscure micro-cap ($40M market cap in early 2024) to a $1.4B strategic critical minerals company. The restart of the Stibnite Hill mine in Montana, securing a $248M Department of Defense contract, and building a $354M contract backlog represent a meaningful business model transformation driven by the antimony supply crisis from Chinese export curbs [1][3]. AI Deployment (0/2): No AI deployment or strategy evident. This is a 100-employee mining and smelting operation with no technology product. Market Share (2/2): UAMY operates the only two antimony smelters in North America and positions itself as the only fully integrated antimony producer outside China and Russia — a de facto monopoly on Western domestic antimony production [1][2]. This category-defining position is validated by the $248M DoD contract and $27M Department of War critical minerals award. Pipeline (1/2): Revenue guidance of $125M for FY2026 (up from $39.3M in FY2025) with $354M in multi-year contracts providing strong visibility [1]. Montana mine restart and capacity expansion underway. R&D Intensity (0/1): Minimal R&D — the company competes on resource access and smelting capacity, not product innovation.

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): UAMY articulates a specific and thoughtful purpose centered on securing America's domestic antimony supply chain and reducing dependence on Chinese and Russian critical mineral imports. CEO Evans frames the company's work as essential to national security and defense readiness — this is genuine, connects to a larger purpose, and differentiates from peers [2]. Community Impact (0/2): No specific charitable foundation, community investment programs, or philanthropic initiatives were identified. The company has only 100 employees and is in early-stage growth. Employee Retention (0/2): With only 100 employees and the CFO (Isaak) going on temporary leave with an interim CFO (Winkler) appointed in May 2026, the organization is too small and too new in its current form for meaningful retention data [2]. Insider Buying (2/2): Significant insider buying by multiple insiders: CEO Gary Evans purchased 100,000 shares for $613,200 in September 2025 and 200,000 shares for $289,400 in December 2024. Director Michael McManus purchased 70,000 shares for $102,060 (December 2024) and 50,000 shares for $28,250 (November 2024) [4]. This represents meaningful personal conviction from leadership.

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics. D/E of 0.17% is essentially zero — the company has virtually no debt, scoring maximum [5]. Revenue growth is extraordinary at 131.4% YoY (FY2025 revenue of $39.3M, up 163% from $14.9M) and estimated 5-year CAGR of 52.7%, both scoring maximum [1]. However, the company remains unprofitable with -11.1% profit margin and -5.1% ROE, both scoring 0. Forward P/E of 73.6 exceeds the 60 floor, scoring 0 — reflecting extreme speculative valuation on a company guiding to $125M revenue [1]. PEG ratio is unavailable and excluded. Weighted calculation (excluding PEG) yields 4.0/8 = 5. Liquidity passes: $9.57 x 12.6M = $120.7M.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (8/9): Antimony mining, smelting, and refining are physical-world operations that AI structurally cannot displace [1]. Antimony is a critical mineral used in flame retardants, ammunition primers, lead-acid batteries, and military applications — all physical products with no AI substitution pathway. The supply crisis is driven by Chinese export restrictions, not technology disruption. AI actually increases demand for antimony indirectly through data center construction (flame retardant applications in electronics) and military modernization (ammunition primers). UAMY's monopoly position as the only integrated Western antimony producer creates a structural moat that is geopolitical, not technological. AI Opportunity (0/1): No AI-related revenue or products. UAMY is a mining and smelting operation with no technology component [2].

infinite_game.summary: UAMY under CEO Gary Evans shows elements of infinite game thinking — the company's purpose of securing American antimony independence is genuine and bigger than the company itself. Evans's personal investment ($900K+ in insider purchases) demonstrates conviction. However, the company is a 100-employee micro-cap that has existed since 1968 but only recently became strategically relevant due to Chinese export restrictions. The organization has not been tested through adversity in its current form, the CFO departure creates governance concerns, and the company's success is more a function of geopolitical circumstance than decades of purposeful building.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): Securing America's domestic antimony supply chain and reducing dependence on Chinese/Russian critical minerals is a genuine Just Cause that is resilient (it will outlast any single leader), service-oriented (it serves national security), idealistic (supply chain independence is a future state not yet achieved), and big (the critical minerals challenge extends beyond antimony) [1][2]. However, the cause emerged from geopolitical circumstance (Chinese export bans) rather than decades of purposeful building, and it remains untested through a period where antimony prices or government support might decline.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): CEO Evans's decision to join UAMY in December 2024 — a near-zero micro-cap — and immediately invest $900K+ of personal capital represents meaningful courage [2][4]. The Montana mine restart, capacity expansion investments, and pursuit of the $248M DoD contract required betting on government support that was not guaranteed. However, the company has not yet been tested by adversity — rising antimony prices and government critical minerals policy have provided tailwinds since Evans's arrival.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (0/2): Governance concerns are significant for a company of this size. The CFO (Isaak) went on temporary leave in May 2026 requiring an interim CFO appointment — leadership instability at a 100-employee company [2]. The organization is too small and too new in its current leadership form (Evans joined December 2024) to have established a culture of trust. No employee sentiment data exists for a company this small.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): UAMY has pivoted from a sleepy micro-cap mining operation to a strategic national security supplier, restarting dormant mines, securing multi-hundred-million-dollar government contracts, and positioning as the Western world's integrated antimony producer [1][3]. This represents meaningful strategic adaptation to a changed environment. However, the pivot was driven by external circumstance (Chinese export bans) rather than proactive reinvention, and the company has not abandoned an existing business — it scaled an existing one.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): UAMY's competitive positioning is framed as an adversarial narrative against Chinese and Russian antimony suppliers rather than learning from rivals' strengths [1][3]. The company's value proposition is defined by what competitors cannot do (supply the US market) rather than by excellence in its own operations. The Reuters article dispute — publicly attacking a media outlet as 'completely inaccurate and misleading' — reflects a defensive posture rather than the self-improvement focus Sinek values.

investment_thesis: United States Antimony is a rare critical minerals play offering monopoly positioning as the only fully integrated antimony producer outside China and Russia. Revenue surged 163% to $39.3M in FY2025 driven by a 230% spike in antimony prices from Chinese export curbs, and the company guides for $125M in FY2026 revenue backed by a $354M contract backlog including a $248M DoD contract [1]. The company has virtually no debt (D/E of 0.17%), significant insider buying from CEO Evans ($900K+), and operates in a critical minerals category with bipartisan political support and direct national security relevance [4]. The Montana Stibnite Hill mine restart and $27M Department of War critical minerals award provide additional growth catalysts [2][3]. At the current $1.4B market cap, UAMY is priced for significant execution on the $125M revenue target.
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on execution risk and valuation for what remains a 100-employee micro-cap mining operation attempting to scale revenue 3x in a single year. Forward P/E of 73.6 prices in flawless execution on contracts that have not yet been fully delivered [5]. The company remains unprofitable with -11.1% profit margin and -5.1% ROE, meaning profitability at scale is unproven. The CFO departure in May 2026 creates governance risk at a critical growth inflection point [2]. Antimony prices are elevated due to Chinese export restrictions that could be reversed through trade negotiations — any normalization of Chinese supply would collapse pricing and the revenue thesis. The company's 58-year history prior to 2024 was characterized by marginal operations and minimal revenue, raising questions about whether management can execute at the scale the backlog demands. Environmental compliance at the Montana mine site requires ongoing regulatory management that could delay production.
key_catalysts: FY2026 revenue tracking toward $125M guidance and first profitable quarter demonstrating operational scalability from $39.3M FY2025 base
Department of Defense $248M contract deliveries beginning to generate meaningful revenue, validating the company's strategic supplier position for US national security
Montana Stibnite Hill mine reaching full production capacity, providing vertically integrated domestic ore supply that eliminates reliance on imported feedstock
key_risks: Execution risk scaling from $39.3M to $125M revenue with only 100 employees — the company has never operated at this scale, and the CFO departure creates leadership gaps at a critical inflection point
Antimony price collapse if Chinese export restrictions are reversed through trade negotiations, eliminating the 230% price premium that drove FY2025 revenue growth

Forward P/E of 73.6 on a still-unprofitable company creates extreme valuation risk — any miss on revenue guidance or contract delivery could trigger severe multiple compression from the $1.4B market cap
